Q1: I usually tried to organize my studying around hands-on practice sessions, since I learn best by doing things rather than just reading. I'd block out chunks of time, maybe three hours at a stretch, where I could really dig into problems and work through exercises physically. My routine wasn't always perfect though—I'd sometimes get overwhelmed by anxiety and have to step back, then push harder later to catch up. I kept a rough schedule in my head, aiming for about 18 hours a week, but honestly it felt like a constant battle between what I planned and what my stress levels allowed.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and how anxious I was feeling that week. When deadlines approached, I'd cram more intensely, sometimes studying late into the night even though I knew it wasn't ideal. On calmer weeks, I tried to spread things out more evenly, but with five online courses running simultaneously, something always seemed to demand urgent attention. The regularity I wanted kept getting disrupted by competing pressures and my own fear of falling behind.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if new materials had been posted or seeing if there was feedback on something I'd submitted. I logged in pretty frequently—probably most days of the week—because I was always worried I might miss something important. Sometimes the anxiety would just push me to check even when I knew nothing had changed. It became almost compulsive at points, like I needed to reassure myself that I was still on track.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became pretty automatic after the first few weeks. I'd wake up, grab coffee, and check the LMS before doing anything else—it was like checking my phone for messages. The habit formed because I was scared of missing announcements or falling behind without realizing it. Once it became routine, it actually helped reduce some of my anxiety, knowing I was staying connected to what was happening in the course.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful for me—I watched nearly three hours worth because I could pause, rewind, and really work through concepts at my own pace. I also found the peer interactions valuable; even though I only posted twice in discussions, I had quite a few exchanges with classmates that helped clarify things. Anything that let me actively engage rather than passively read worked better for my kinesthetic learning style. The practical exercises connected to videos were gold for me.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped or skimmed the longer reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. They felt too passive for how I learn, and when anxiety was high, sitting still to read dense text just made me more restless. I also delayed some of the optional discussion prompts because they felt less urgent than the assignments I was already struggling to complete. It wasn't that I thought they were useless—I just had to prioritize, and my assignment completion rate shows I was already stretched thin.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to exam performance or when it involved actually doing something rather than just absorbing information. If I could practice a skill or work through a problem, I was all in. I also valued anything that reduced my uncertainty about what would be tested. When I finished something and felt like I genuinely understood it better, that sense of progress made the time investment feel justified.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford. With required work already overwhelming me and my assignment completion hovering around 30%, I couldn't justify spending energy on extras unless they seemed directly helpful for exams. Required activities carried this weight of consequence that optional ones didn't—the fear of failing pushed me through required work even when I was exhausted. Optional stuff felt like it was for students who had their lives more together than I did.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and worked through them on paper, making my own notes and diagrams that I could physically manipulate. I used external YouTube videos to supplement concepts I was struggling with, and I had a couple of textbooks I'd flip through when the online explanations weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened with materials spread across my desk, completely offline, where I could move things around and really engage kinesthetically.
Q10: There were many evenings where I'd print out problem sets and work through them by hand for hours, never touching the LMS during that time. I'd also meet up with a couple of classmates in person to work through difficult concepts together—those peer interactions wouldn't show up in my log at all. Sometimes I'd watch a video once online, then replay the concepts in my head while going for a walk or doing something physical. The LMS only captured the starting point, not the whole journey.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd download PDFs and video transcripts, then work with them offline for days. Once I had the material, I didn't need to keep going back to the LMS—I'd annotate printouts, create flashcards, and practice problems all without logging in again. My high video watch time reflects initial viewing, but a lot of my real engagement with that content happened afterward, away from the screen, where I could process it in ways that suited how I learn.
Q12: There were definitely low points, especially mid-semester when multiple deadlines collided and my anxiety spiked hard. I remember one week where I felt genuinely scared—like paralyzed scared—about whether I could keep up with everything. The CBT sessions I attended helped me work through some of that, but in those moments, even logging into the LMS felt overwhelming. My mood would tank, and I'd have to force myself to engage even minimally.
Q13: Workload absolutely affected my LMS usage. When I was juggling five courses and feeling that medium-high stress, I'd sometimes just log in to check deadlines without actually engaging with content. Fatigue made me less thorough—I'd skim instead of study properly. Competing responsibilities, like part-time work and just basic life stuff, meant the LMS sometimes got my leftover energy rather than my best focus. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those sections temporarily.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open videos or readings and just... not absorb anything. My eyes would move across the screen but my mind was somewhere else, usually spiraling about deadlines or worrying about my performance. I'd technically be 'active' in the LMS, but I wasn't learning. Those sessions felt hollow—like I was going through motions to convince myself I was studying when really I was too anxious to focus properly.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my completion rate was so low despite my exam scores being high. The anxiety made starting things feel impossible sometimes—I'd know an assignment was important but feel paralyzed by fear of doing it wrong. I'd avoid it until the pressure became unbearable, then either rush through it or miss the deadline entirely. It's frustrating because I knew what I needed to do; my brain just wouldn't cooperate.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. They'd trigger intense anxiety, but they also forced me to finally engage when I'd been avoiding things. My LMS activity would spike dramatically in the days before something was due—I'd suddenly log in constantly, watch videos I'd been putting off, and frantically try to catch up. Without deadlines, I might have spread my effort more evenly, but honestly, the fear of missing them was sometimes the only thing that got me moving.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Good feedback boosted my confidence and made me more willing to engage with similar materials. But waiting for feedback was torture—I'd check the LMS obsessively, which inflated my login frequency. When feedback was critical, even constructively so, it sometimes triggered my anxiety and made me avoid that type of work for a while. I needed feedback to improve, but receiving it was emotionally costly.
Q18: The course structure helped in some ways—having clear modules and deadlines gave me a framework to hang my chaotic study habits on. But the pacing sometimes felt relentless, especially with five courses running simultaneously. When the structure allowed flexibility, I could adapt to my anxiety levels. When it was rigid, I'd fall behind and then struggle to catch up. The weekly rhythm eventually became familiar, which helped reduce some uncertainty.
Q19: The video-heavy approach really worked for me since I could engage actively with that format. Having materials downloadable was crucial—it let me study in ways that suited my kinesthetic style. What made engagement harder was when there were too many small, scattered tasks that felt administrative rather than educational. Also, discussion requirements felt forced sometimes; I prefer learning through doing rather than writing posts. The task completion tracking stressed me out when I saw my percentage lagging.
Q20: I think that pattern might reflect students like me who are exploring, watching, and trying to understand the material but struggling to actually complete and submit work. High anxiety can make you engage obsessively with content while simultaneously making it impossible to finish assignments. You might also be someone who learns a lot through the LMS but demonstrates that knowledge through exams rather than submitted work. Activity doesn't equal output when mental health is a factor.
Q21: That definitely resonates with my experience. My exam score was over 95%, but my assignment completion was barely 30%—the LMS wouldn't show how much I learned offline or through methods that didn't leave digital traces. Some students are efficient; they don't need to constantly revisit materials because they absorb things quickly or study effectively outside the system. Low visible activity doesn't mean low engagement—it might mean different engagement.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns—when someone is active, what they access, how often they return to materials. They're good at capturing digital breadcrumbs. But they miss everything that happens offline, the quality of engagement versus just time spent, and the emotional reality behind the numbers. They can't tell if someone watched a video while fully focused or while having a panic attack. They show behavior but not understanding or struggle.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm genuinely wrestling with the material, not just exposing myself to it. It's when I'm thinking about course concepts while doing other things, when I'm curious about topics beyond what's required, when I feel connected to the learning process even when it's hard. Engagement isn't about hours logged or videos watched—it's about whether the content is actually changing how I think. For me, it often involves physical activity and hands-on practice.
Q24: The LMS can't show the fear I felt almost constantly, or how hard I had to fight through anxiety just to complete basic tasks. It can't capture the CBT sessions that kept me functional, or the offline study that actually produced my learning. It doesn't show the peer conversations that happened in person, or the moments I stared at my ceiling processing concepts before falling asleep. My distinction grade came from resilience and struggle that no log could ever quantify.
